probe_designs.probe_name: Per-Attribute Label Imbalance & Prior Shift Audit
probe_designs.content: CelebA attributes are heavily imbalanced (e.g., 'Bald' ~2%, 'No_Beard' ~83%, 'Wearing_Hat' ~5%). Compute positive prevalence p_a for each of the 40 attributes on train/val/test splits. Compare base rates across splits to detect prior shift. Additionally, compute the model's predicted positive rate per attribute and compare against the empirical base rate. A healthy signal: predicted rates closely track empirical rates (within a few percentage points) and train/val/test priors match. Unhealthy: severe divergence (model never predicts rare attributes like Bald/Mustache), or train/test base-rate gaps >2%. Use pos_weight in BCEWithLogitsLoss or focal loss if imbalance dominates learning. Also flag attributes with Dice < 0.1 or F1 approaching zero despite high accuracy — these are symptoms of majority-class collapse.
possible_sources: Liu et al., 'Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild' (ICCV 2015, CelebA)
Lin et al., 'Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection' (ICCV 2017)
Huang et al., 'Learning Deep Representation for Imbalanced Classification' (CVPR 2016)

probe_designs.content: Slice test set by the 'Male' and 'Young' attributes (and a proxy for skin tone using 'Pale_Skin' or external FairFace labels) to form demographic subgroups. Compute per-attribute accuracy, F1, TPR, FPR within each subgroup. Report Equal Opportunity Difference (ΔTPR) and Demographic Parity Difference per attribute. Healthy: |ΔTPR| < 0.05 across subgroups for most attributes. Unhealthy: large gaps (e.g., 'Attractive' predicted much more for Young+Female; 'Smiling' detection degraded on darker skin tones). Cross-reference with known CelebA biases and Gender Shades findings.
possible_sources: Buolamwini & Gebru, 'Gender Shades' (FAT* 2018)
Kärkkäinen & Joo, 'FairFace' (WACV 2021)
Ramaswamy et al., 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing' (CVPR 2021)
Denton et al., 'Detecting Bias with Generative Counterfactual Face Attribute Augmentation' (2019)

probe_designs.content: After sigmoid, bin predicted probabilities (e.g., 15 bins) and compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Brier score per attribute. Plot reliability diagrams. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 per attribute, diagonal reliability curves. Unhealthy: systematic over/under-confidence, especially on rare attributes where BCE with imbalance tends to push sigmoids toward 0. If miscalibrated, apply temperature scaling or Platt scaling per-attribute on a held-out val set. Also compare optimal per-attribute decision threshold vs default 0.5 — large deviations indicate calibration/imbalance issues.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Naeini et al., 'Obtaining Well Calibrated Probabilities Using Bayesian Binning' (AAAI 2015)
Kull et al., 'Beyond Temperature Scaling' (NeurIPS 2019)

probe_designs.content: Track BCE loss, per-attribute AUROC, AUPRC, F1, and mean Average Precision (mAP) across epochs for train/val/test. Inspect gap = train − val metrics. Healthy: val loss decreases monotonically then plateaus, train-val gap < 10% relative, best-val epoch aligns with best-test epoch. Unhealthy: val loss rising while train loss falls (overfitting), erratic oscillations (too-high LR), or val plateauing far below train (capacity/regularization mismatch). Use early stopping on val mAP. Also inspect per-attribute learning curves — some attributes (e.g., 'Blurry', 'Attractive') may overfit earlier than others.
possible_sources: Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville, 'Deep Learning' Ch. 7
Prechelt, 'Early Stopping — But When?' (1998)
He et al., 'Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition' (CVPR 2016)

probe_designs.content: Evaluate the model on face images corrupted with Gaussian noise, blur (motion/defocus), JPEG compression, brightness/contrast shifts, and occlusion (CutOut patches on eyes/mouth). Compute mean Corruption Error (mCE) and per-attribute accuracy drop vs. clean. Healthy: graceful degradation (<10% absolute drop at severity 3 for most attributes). Unhealthy: catastrophic collapse on blur/noise, particularly for fine-grained attributes ('Arched_Eyebrows', 'Pointy_Nose'). Also probe with face-specific perturbations: sunglasses overlay should flip 'Eyeglasses' only; other attributes should remain stable (causal attribute isolation).
possible_sources: Hendrycks & Dietterich, 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations' (ICLR 2019, ImageNet-C)
Karahan et al., 'How Image Degradations Affect Deep CNN-based Face Recognition?' (BIOSIG 2016)
DeVries & Taylor, 'Cutout' (2017)

probe_designs.content: CelebA attributes are strongly correlated (e.g., 'Heavy_Makeup' ↔ 'Wearing_Lipstick' ↔ 'Female'; 'Goatee' ↔ 'Male'; 'Wearing_Necktie' ↔ 'Male'). Compute the 40×40 attribute correlation matrix on training labels, then compute the corresponding 40×40 correlation of model predictions on test. Flag attributes whose prediction correlation structure exceeds the label correlation structure — these indicate the model relies on co-occurrence rather than visual evidence. Additionally, evaluate on counterfactual/rare combinations (e.g., Male+Heavy_Makeup, Female+Goatee) drawn from test; a healthy model should predict each attribute independently from its true visual cue, not the correlated group.
possible_sources: Ramaswamy et al., 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing' (CVPR 2021)
Sagawa et al., 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks for Group Shifts' (ICLR 2020, Group DRO)
Singh et al., 'Don't Judge an Object by Its Context' (CVPR 2020)

probe_designs.content: For each of the 40 output logits, compute Grad-CAM / Score-CAM heatmaps on a curated set of faces. Expected regions: 'Eyeglasses' → eyes; 'Smiling'/'Mouth_Slightly_Open' → mouth; 'Bangs'/'Bald' → forehead/hairline; 'Wearing_Necktie' → neck. Quantify via pointing-game accuracy against facial-landmark bounding regions. Healthy: high pointing-game score (>70%) for localizable attributes. Unhealthy: saliency on background, hair, or clothes for attributes that should be local (suggests shortcut learning). Flag any attribute where mean saliency-face-overlap < 0.3.
possible_sources: Selvaraju et al., 'Grad-CAM' (ICCV 2017)
Zhang et al., 'Top-down Neural Attention by Excitation Backprop' (ECCV 2016, pointing game)
Fong & Vedaldi, 'Interpretable Explanations of Black Boxes by Meaningful Perturbation' (ICCV 2017)

probe_designs.content: Run L∞-bounded PGD and FGSM attacks (ε ∈ {2/255, 4/255, 8/255}) targeting individual attribute logits. Measure attack success rate and mean flip count per image. Also evaluate against semantic adversarial perturbations (small brightness/rotation). Healthy: low single-attribute flip rate at small ε for robustly visual attributes (Eyeglasses, Wearing_Hat). Unhealthy: cross-attribute collateral flips (perturbing 'Smiling' also flips 'Attractive'), which reveals entangled features. Compare clean vs. adversarial accuracy per attribute; large drops indicate poor feature robustness.
possible_sources: Madry et al., 'Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks' (ICLR 2018)
Goodfellow et al., 'Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples' (ICLR 2015)
Rozsa et al., 'Are Facial Attributes Adversarially Robust?' (ICPR 2016)

probe_designs.content: During training, log per-layer gradient norm, weight norm, update/weight ratio, and BN running stats. Also track per-attribute logit magnitude and per-attribute loss contribution (since BCE sums 40 terms, some can dominate). Healthy: update/weight ratio ~1e-3, stable gradient norm, no NaN/Inf, balanced per-attribute loss contribution after few epochs. Unhealthy: exploding gradients (LR too high), vanishing gradients in early layers (frozen/dead backbone), or a single attribute (e.g., 'Attractive') contributing >20% of total loss persistently — suggests label noise or insufficient capacity for that attribute.
possible_sources: Karpathy, 'A Recipe for Training Neural Networks' (2019 blog)
Ioffe & Szegedy, 'Batch Normalization' (ICML 2015)
Glorot & Bengio, 'Understanding the Difficulty of Training Deep Feedforward Networks' (AISTATS 2010)

probe_designs.content: CelebA has documented label noise especially for subjective attributes ('Attractive', 'Big_Lips', 'Oval_Face', 'Young', 'Pointy_Nose'). Use model disagreement with labels on a high-confidence subset, cross-validation prediction confidence, Cleanlab/confident learning, or loss-based ranking (AUM, Forgetting Events) to surface suspected mislabeled samples. Compute per-attribute estimated noise rate. Healthy: noise rate <5% for objective attributes (Eyeglasses, Wearing_Hat, Male), higher and acknowledged for subjective ones. Unhealthy: objective attributes with >10% estimated mislabel rate — inspect annotator guidelines and consider relabeling or sample-weighting. Also detect duplicate/near-duplicate images across train/test (identity leakage) via perceptual hashing.
possible_sources: Northcutt et al., 'Confident Learning' (JAIR 2021, Cleanlab)
Pleiss et al., 'Identifying Mislabeled Data Using the Area Under the Margin Ranking' (NeurIPS 2020, AUM)
Toneva et al., 'An Empirical Study of Example Forgetting' (ICLR 2019)
Lingenfelter et al., 'A Quantitative Analysis of Labeling Issues in the CelebA Dataset' (ISVC 2022)
